content: 36 cells each of internal resistance $0.5 \Omega$ and emf 1.5 V each are used to send current through an external circuit of $2 \Omega$ resistor. For getting maximum current, $n$ cells are combined in series in each row. If total number of such rows are $m$, then the value of $(n / m)$ will be
explanation:
Given:

emf, $E = 1.5 \, \text{V}$

internal resistance per cell, $r = 0.5 \, \Omega$

external resistance, $R = 2 \, \Omega$

total number of cells, $mn = 36$

For optimal current flow using a mixed arrangement of cells:

The relationship between the internal resistance of each series connection and the total external resistance is described by:

$ \frac{nr}{m} = R $

Plugging in the given values:

$ \frac{n \times 0.5}{m} = 2 $

Multiplying the equations for the total number of cells and the condition for maximum current gives:

$ 0.5n^2 = 72 $

Solving for $n$:

$ n^2 = 144 $

$ n = 12 $

$ m = \frac{36}{12} = 3 $

Therefore:

$ \frac{n}{m} = \frac{12}{3} = 4 $

content: The maximum power rating of a $20 \Omega$ resistor is 2 kW . Then, it cannot be used with 300 V DC source because the

options.content: current drawn is very large and it can blow the fuse.
current drawn is very less and appliance does not work.
resistor may melt due to excessive heat.

explanation:
The maximum power rating of a $20 \Omega$ resistor is 2 kW. This resistor should not be used with a 300 V DC source because it would exceed its power rating, potentially damaging the resistor due to excessive heat.

Explanation:

Maximum Power Rating: The resistor's maximum power rating is 2 kW.

Calculating Power Dissipation:

When connected to a 300 V DC source, the power dissipated across the resistor is calculated using the formula:

$ P = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{300^2}{20} $

Calculation:

$ P = \frac{300 \times 300}{20} = 4500 \, \text{W} $

$ P = 4.5 \, \text{kW} $

Comparison:

Since 4.5 kW > 2 kW, the power dissipation would exceed the resistor's maximum power rating.

Therefore, the resistor is at risk of being damaged because it would produce excessive heat beyond its tolerance.

content: The magnitude of resistance $X$ in the circuit shown below in the figure, when no current flows through the $5 \Omega$ resistor, is
explanation:
As no current flows through the middle $5 \Omega$ resistor, the circuit represents a balanced Wheatstone bridge.

$$\therefore \quad \frac{X}{18}=\frac{2}{6} \text { or } X=\frac{2}{6} \times 18=6 \Omega$$

content: A constant voltage is applied between the two ends of a uniform metallic wire, Some heat is developed in it. The heat developed is halved if

options.content: both the length and the radius of the wire are halved
both the length and the radius of the wire are doubled.
The radius of the wire is doubled
The length of the wire is halved.

explanation:
The heat produced is given by,

$$H=\frac{V^2}{R} \text { and } R=\frac{1}{\pi r^2}, H=\left(\pi V^2\right) \frac{r^2}{l}$$

Thus, heat is halved if length is halved.

content: 24 cells of emf $$1.5 \mathrm{~V}$$ each having internal resistance of $$1 \mathrm{~ohm}$$ are connected to an external resistance of $$1.5 \mathrm{~ohms}$$. To get maximum current

options.content: all cells are connected in series combination
all cells are connected in parallel combination
4 cells in each row are connected in series and 6 such rows are connected in parallel
6 cells in each row are connected in series and 4 such rows are connected in parallel

explanation:
For series, $$i=\frac{n E}{n r+R}$$

$$i=\frac{24 \times 1.5}{24+1.5}=1.41$$

For parallel, $$i=\frac{E}{\frac{r}{n}+R}$$

$$i=\frac{1.5}{\frac{1}{24}+1.5}=0.97$$

For $$4 \times 6$$ combination

$$\begin{aligned} & i=\frac{n E}{R+\frac{n r}{m}} \\ & i=\frac{4 \times 1.5}{1.5+\frac{4}{6}}=2.77 \end{aligned}$$

For $$6 \times 4$$ combination

$$\begin{aligned} i & =\frac{n E}{R+\frac{n r}{M}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad i & =\frac{6 \times 1.5 \times 4}{12}=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: A silver wire of radius $$0.1 \mathrm{~cm}$$ carries a current of $$2 \mathrm{~A}$$. If the charge density in silver is $$5.86 \times 10^{28} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$$, the drift velocity is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \quad v_d=\frac{I}{\pi A e} \\ & \text { Given, } I=2 \mathrm{~A}, n=5.86 \times 10^{28} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}, \\ & A=\pi r^2=\pi\left(0.1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2, e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C} \\ & \therefore \quad v_d=0.68 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

content: An electron revolves in a circle at the rate of $$10^{19}$$ rounds per second. The equivalent current is $$\left(e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$$
explanation:
The equivalent current produced by an electron revolving in a circle can be calculated by taking into account the charge of the electron and the rate at which it makes rounds per second.

The formula to calculate current ($$I$$) in this context is given by:

$I = n\cdot e$

where:

  • $$n$$ is the number of electrons passing a given point per second, and in this case, it's equivalent to the number of rounds made by a single electron per second because it's only one electron.
  • $$e$$ is the charge of an electron ($$1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{Coulombs}$$).

Given that the electron makes $$10^{19}$$ rounds per second, we have:

$n = 10^{19} \, \text{rounds per second}$

Therefore, to find the current, we substitute $$n$$ and $$e$$ into the formula:

$I = n\cdot e = 10^{19} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$

$I = 1.6 \, \text{A}$

So, the equivalent current is 1.6 A. Hence, the correct answer is Option B: 1.6 A.

content: The temperature coefficient of the resistance of a wire is 0.00125 per $${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$. At $$300 \mathrm{~K}$$ its resistance is $$1 \Omega$$. The resistance of wire will be $$2 \Omega$$ at
explanation:
Note None of the four options are correct, through option (d) is close to correct answer.

$$\begin{aligned} R_t & =R_0(1+\alpha t) \\ 1 & =R_0(1+0.00125 \times 27) \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ 2 & =R_0(1+0.00125 \times t) \quad \text{.... (ii)}\end{aligned}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii)

$$t=848^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1121 \mathrm{~K}$$
